summary: I was born in Burma, but I don't really remember much about it because I was just a baby when my parents fled the war and Burmese soldiers from our village. Most of my family still lives in villages in Burma, and sometimes they come to Thailand to visit. Burma is where my roots are, but I grew up mostly in Thailand.

I grew up in Thailand for about 15 years before moving to the United States. I lived in refugee camps there, specifically in the Mila camp, which was made of bamboo houses without electricity for most people. Life in the camps was hard but manageable; we had some schooling, food provided by the UN and Thai people, and limited access to technology like old Nokia phones. Thailand was where I spent my childhood and youth before resettling.

I moved to the United States when I was about 15 years old after applying through the UN resettlement program. Before coming here, I went through interviews and medical checkups, and they prepared me by teaching about life in America, like how to call 911 and what a bathroom is. Life here is very different from the camps in Thailand, with better schools, food, and access to technology. The US became my new home where I could start fresh.

Mila is the refugee camp in Thailand where I grew up. It was made mostly of bamboo and wood houses with roofs made from leaves, and most people didn’t have electricity unless they were wealthy. The camp was crowded and basic, with limited medical care and food provided by the UN and Thai people. Mila was my home for many years before I moved to the United States.

The United Nations played a big role in my life while I was in the refugee camp in Thailand. They helped by bringing food like rice and fish paste to the camps and organizing resettlement programs. The UN came to the camp to ask if we wanted to move to countries like the United States, and they handled the application and interview process. Without the UN, I probably wouldn’t have had the chance to start a new life in America.

When I was growing up in the refugee camp in Thailand, we didn’t have internet or social media like Facebook. It was only after moving to the United States that I started to learn about and use Facebook and other online platforms. Back then, phones were basic Nokia models without internet access, so Facebook was not part of my life until later.
cues: learned Facebook after moving to US

summary: In the refugee camps in Thailand, the phones we had were mostly old Nokia phones. They were basic and didn’t have internet or social media capabilities. People used them mainly to make calls, sometimes with SIM cards they had to buy to connect internationally. Nokia phones were a big part of communication in the camps before smartphones became common.

Japan was mentioned briefly in the context of places refugees could apply to resettle, along with the United States, Canada, and Europe. However, I personally did not apply to or move to Japan. It was one of the options presented by the UN during the resettlement process in the refugee camps.

New York City was part of my journey after arriving in the United States. I remember landing at John F. Kennedy International Airport, which is in New York City, when I first came here. NYC was the gateway to my new life in America, and it was a big change from the refugee camps in Thailand.

When I moved to the United States, I arrived through John F. Kennedy International Airport in New York City. It was my first experience stepping into America, and everything felt very new and different from the refugee camps. JFK was the starting point of my new life here.

Syracuse, New York is where I settled after moving to the United States. It was quite different from the refugee camps and even New York City, with better schools and more opportunities. Living in Syracuse helped me adjust to life in America and continue my education.
